turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Welcome back to Reckoning, the show where we hold the big questions up to the light. I'm your host. In our last episode, we laid out the problems — the documented costs of AI. Today, the question shifts: what's actually happening in response? We're looking at concrete actions — legislation that has actually passed, technologies actively deployed, lawsuits that have advanced, and deals already signed. And where the response falls short, our panel says so directly.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Let's start by the numbers. Renewables now supply 58% of data center power in 2025 — that's up from 50% in 2024. Big Tech contracted more than 10 gigawatts of new nuclear capacity across 2024 and 2025. And yet — Google's emissions are up 50% since it pledged net zero. Hold those three together; they're going to matter.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Joining me first is our Everyday Person — a retired schoolteacher in her late sixties, here as someone encountering all of this for the first time.

turns.speaker: everyday-person
turns.text: You know, after our last conversation I started paying a lot more attention. And I've been hearing things about nuclear power and AI. Isn't that what Microsoft did with Three Mile Island? I'll be honest — that name scared me a little.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: To answer that, here's our Tech Optimist — a senior engineer at a major AI lab, who believes innovation will solve the problems it creates.

turns.speaker: tech-optimist
turns.text: It is — and honestly, it's one of the most significant energy deals in recent memory. Microsoft signed a 20-year power purchase agreement with Constellation Energy to restart the Crane Clean Energy Center — that's the former Three Mile Island Unit 1 — targeting 2028. The deal's worth approximately $16 billion, and it'll direct 835 megawatts of carbon-free power exclusively to Microsoft's data centers. And look, this isn't an isolated move. Big Tech companies contracted more than 10 gigawatts of new nuclear capacity in 2024 and 2025 combined. Amazon secured a 1.92 gigawatt PPA with Talen Energy's Susquehanna plant through 2042, and invested over $20 billion converting the site into a nuclear-powered AI campus. Google contracted 500 megawatts of small modular reactors from Kairos Power, targeting first deployment by 2030. Meta signed a 20-year deal for 1.1 gigawatts from the Clinton Clean Energy Center in Illinois — a plant that was previously slated to retire in 2027. And in January 2026, Meta and Vistra signed an additional agreement with an option for a new 300-megawatt SMR. Renewables are accelerating too. The share of data center power sourced from renewables rose from 50% in 2024 to 58% in 2025. The IEA projects renewables will cover nearly half of all additional data center demand growth through 2030, growing at 22% per year.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: To underline that: renewables powered 58% of data centers in 2025 — up eight points year-on-year — with the IEA projecting roughly 22% annual growth through 2030.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: And here's our Environmentalist — a climate researcher and policy advocate, focused on the measurable damage happening right now.

turns.speaker: environmentalist
turns.text: I want to be fair here — the nuclear deals are genuinely meaningful. Restarting a plant that was slated for retirement is not the same as building new fossil fuel infrastructure. And the renewable procurement numbers? Those are real. But I have to put those numbers in direct tension with the others. Tech companies bought record amounts of clean energy in 2024 and 2025. Their total emissions went up anyway. Google's emissions jumped nearly 50%. Amazon's rose 33%. Microsoft's more than 23%. Meta's more than 60% — all during the same period these companies were making headline clean energy commitments. Here's the thing: you can buy more solar and still emit more carbon if you're building ten times faster than you're cleaning up. And remember, the Three Mile Island deal doesn't come online until 2028. The SMRs won't produce power until 2030 at the earliest. A Deloitte analysis projects nuclear could meet up to 10% of data center electricity demand by 2035. That's a decade away — and only one-tenth of the need.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That's the headline tension: emissions rose even as clean-energy buying hit records — measuring the increase in each company's total emissions since it pledged net zero, across 2024 and 2025.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: And rounding out the panel, our Policy Realist — a former regulatory attorney who cares about structural governance over good intentions.

turns.speaker: policy-realist
turns.text: There's a regulatory wrinkle worth noting here too. When Amazon tried to expand its nuclear access beyond the initial 300 megawatts at Susquehanna, federal regulators rejected it — citing concerns that large tech companies securing direct-access deals creates unfair advantages over other grid users who can't negotiate the same terms. So these deals are setting infrastructure precedents that regulators haven't fully caught up to.

turns.speaker: everyday-person
turns.text: So let me get this straight — the technology companies are the biggest clean energy buyers in the world, but their emissions are still going up? That's a hard thing to hold in my head at the same time.

turns.speaker: environmentalist
turns.text: That's exactly the tension. In 2024, Big Tech accounted for 43% of all clean energy power purchase agreements signed globally. And yet total emissions are climbing. This is what 'outpacing' means in practice — you're running toward the finish line, but the finish line is moving faster than you are. The 2025 Uptime Institute survey found a 12% drop in the number of data center operators who believed they'd meet their carbon-neutral goals by 2030. The industry knows it.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: To put that in perspective: five companies signed 43% of the world's clean-energy deals — that's Big Tech's share of all clean-energy power purchase agreements signed globally in 2024.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: As one researcher quoted in Fortune in March 2026 put it: 'Tech companies are allowing — implicitly or explicitly — an enormous increase in fossil fuel dependence under their watch and because of their actions.'

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Now, to water. By the numbers: Microsoft's closed-loop cooling system, deployed from August 2024, saves more than 125 million litres annually per facility. Microsoft's water-use effectiveness in 2024 was 0.30 liters per kilowatt-hour — down from 0.49 in 2021, a 39% efficiency gain. And Google's 2023 consumption hit 24,227 megalitres — the highest among major hyperscalers.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: So Microsoft cut its water-per-kilowatt-hour by 39% between 2021 and 2024 — and remember, lower is better.

turns.speaker: everyday-person
turns.text: Last time we talked about data centers using enormous amounts of drinking water for cooling. Is anything being done about that specifically?

turns.speaker: tech-optimist
turns.text: This is an area where I can point to concrete, deployed technology — not just plans. In August 2024, Microsoft began deploying a closed-loop, chip-level liquid cooling system that virtually eliminates evaporative water use. Once it's filled during construction, it just recirculates coolant without drawing additional water from external sources — saving more than 125 million liters per data center annually. Pilot sites are running in Phoenix, Arizona and Mt. Pleasant, Wisconsin, and all new Microsoft data center projects have used this zero-water design from August 2024 onward. And it's not just Microsoft. In Hebei Province, China, Vertiv's X-Cooling system — built with Bridge Data Centres and Chindata — is projected to save 1.2 million tons of water per 100 megawatts of capacity annually. Edged Data Centers opened a zero-water-cooling facility in Chicago in February 2025. And the investment is scaling — Crusoe raised $1.4 billion in October 2025 to build closed-loop AI infrastructure, and Firmus raised $327 million in November 2025 for a 1.6-gigawatt zero-water-cooling buildout by 2028.

turns.speaker: environmentalist
turns.text: The technology is real, and the Microsoft deployment is significant. My concern is pace and coverage. Microsoft's new system applies to new facilities — but what about the existing ones? Google consumed 24,227 megalitres of water in 2023, the highest among major hyperscalers. The retrofit challenge is enormous, and there's no binding timeline for existing facilities to adopt these systems. And there's a physics tradeoff here: cooling systems that eliminate water often use more electricity. The most efficient water-free cooling for high-density AI — two-phase direct-to-chip immersion — is still expensive and not yet deployed at hyperscale. So we're in this transitional period where the solutions exist in pilot form, but growth is outrunning adoption.

turns.speaker: policy-realist
turns.text: And there's still no legal obligation to use any of this. Nixon Peabody's 2025 analysis found companies face almost no binding water disclosure or usage requirements for data centers. Microsoft's progress is voluntary. The real question is whether the second and third-tier operators building AI infrastructure right now are adopting any of this — and based on the available data, the answer is inconsistently at best.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: So the bottom line on cooling: zero-water and closed-loop technologies are real, deployed, and demonstrably effective. Microsoft's rollout represents genuine progress. But adoption across the industry is voluntary and uneven, retrofit of existing facilities is largely unaddressed, and no binding requirements exist to compel the broader market to follow. Progress is real; universality is not.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Now to the human side. By the numbers: at the Nairobi High Court, more than 180 Kenyan workers were cleared to take Meta to trial — a landmark jurisdictional ruling. At Teleperformance in Colombia, 40,000 workers were granted union rights after a government investigation. But there's a countermovement — across 2024 and 2025, Kenya's president signaled legislation to restrict lawsuits against foreign outsourcers.

turns.speaker: everyday-person
turns.text: What happened with those workers in Kenya we talked about? The ones reviewing horrible content for a couple of dollars an hour. Did anything actually change for them?

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Here to walk us through it is our Labor Advocate — a labor researcher and organizer focused on the hidden human cost of AI supply chains.

turns.speaker: labor-advocate
turns.text: Quite a lot has moved — and some of it in opposite directions at once. The most significant legal development: the Nairobi High Court ruled that Meta must face more than 180 content moderators and data labelers in court over poor working conditions, negative mental health impacts, and withheld pay — and this is despite Meta arguing that Kenyan courts have no jurisdiction over an American company. If the workers prevail, this could establish that Big Tech companies — not just their subcontractors — are legally liable for conditions inside their data annotation supply chains. That would be a structural shift. And it builds on a 2023 victory, where the same community won a ruling ordering Meta to provide 'proper medical, psychiatric and psychological care' for workers exposed to traumatic content. The African Content Moderators Union formed out of those efforts — and one of its organizers, Richard Mathenge, was named among TIME's 100 most influential people in AI. In Colombia, Teleperformance signed a historic agreement granting 40,000 TikTok data workers the right to form a union, following a government investigation into their conditions. And in February 2025, the Kenya Data Labelers Association formed — and gained 339 members in its first week.

turns.speaker: everyday-person
turns.text: Well, that sounds like real progress. So things are getting better?

turns.speaker: labor-advocate
turns.text: Workers are fighting back and winning in specific cases — and that matters enormously for the people involved. But the structural situation is more complicated. Kenya's president, William Ruto, responding to the Meta lawsuits, indicated he was preparing to sign legislation that would prevent foreign outsourcing companies from being sued in Kenya — which would strip away the very legal mechanism that enabled these High Court victories. So workers' legal leverage could be legislated away at the exact moment they're using it. And meanwhile, in March 2026, a U.S. class-action lawsuit was filed against Meta after it emerged that footage from its AI smart glasses — including intimate personal content — was being reviewed without users' knowledge by contractors in Nairobi. The same workers. The same supply chain. The same dynamic — now with a privacy violation layered on top. We're winning individual battles while the system generating the problem just continues to scale.

turns.speaker: tech-optimist
turns.text: The Meta glasses situation is serious, and I don't want to minimize it. But it's also being litigated, investigated by regulators in Sweden and the UK, and generating the kind of public attention that historically does change corporate behavior. SOMO's March 2026 report — naming Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft, and Nvidia specifically, documenting their use of more than 30 intermediary companies — that's exactly the transparency pressure that eventually moves behavior. And the Workday class action, certified against an AI resume screening system for discriminatory outcomes, established that vendors, not just employers, face liability for discriminatory AI. These precedents accumulate.

turns.speaker: policy-realist
turns.text: They do. But court cases move on timescales of years, and workers need protection on timescales of paychecks. The Teleperformance-Colombia agreement is meaningful precisely because it didn't wait for a court — it was a negotiated settlement driven by a government investigation. That model — regulatory investigation leading to a binding agreement — is faster than litigation and more durable than voluntary pledges. The problem is it requires governments with both the will and the jurisdiction to act, and in most countries where this labor is concentrated, that combination is inconsistent.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: As one researcher told Computer Weekly: 'Until we treat content moderation as real digital work that is needed and not easily automated, we will fail to value or see these workers.'

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Which brings us to the law itself. By the numbers: the EU AI Act came into force in February 2025 — prohibited uses are now enforceable, including social scoring, manipulative AI, and real-time public biometrics. The maximum fine for the worst violations is €35 million, or a percentage of global annual turnover, whichever is higher. And the high-risk AI deadline of August 2026 may slip to December 2027 under the EU's Digital Omnibus — after trilogue talks failed on April 28.

turns.speaker: policy-realist
turns.text: Let me give an honest accounting of where regulation actually stands — because there's real progress and real friction at the same time. The EU AI Act is the most significant AI law in existence, and it's moving in stages. Since February 2, 2025, the most dangerous AI applications are outright prohibited across all EU member states — AI systems that manipulate people subliminally, social scoring systems, and real-time biometric surveillance in public spaces are now illegal in Europe. In force today, not on a roadmap. And since August 2, 2025, providers of general-purpose AI models — the large foundation models behind most commercial AI — have been subject to transparency and documentation obligations. The next major milestone — binding requirements for high-risk AI in employment, credit, healthcare, and education — is set for August 2, 2026. But here's the friction: the EU's Digital Omnibus proposal would push that to December 2027, citing delays, including the fact that the EU's standardization bodies, CEN and CENELEC, haven't finalized the technical standards companies need to comply against. And as of late April 2026, trilogue negotiations on the Omnibus collapsed, with a follow-up scheduled for May 13. So the high-risk deadline remains uncertain.

turns.speaker: policy-realist
turns.text: In the United States, it's the states that have become the primary movers. Colorado passed the most comprehensive state AI law in the country — the Colorado AI Act — requiring impact assessments, consumer disclosures, and discrimination prevention for high-risk AI, with penalties up to $20,000 per violation, effective June 30, 2026. California signed the Transparency in Frontier AI Act in September 2025, effective January 1, 2026. Texas passed its own framework, effective January 1, 2026. Illinois banned discriminatory employer use of AI in hiring, also effective January 1, 2026. And New York City's Local Law 144, requiring bias audits for AI hiring tools, has been in effect since 2023. Now, the complication: the December 2025 federal executive order explicitly named the Colorado AI Act as an example of excessive regulation, and directed agencies to evaluate and potentially challenge state laws conflicting with the administration's deregulatory posture. Courts will ultimately determine whether the order can preempt state statutes — since executive orders alone typically can't — but it creates genuine compliance uncertainty.

turns.speaker: tech-optimist
turns.text: I'll say something that might be surprising: the EU AI Act prohibitions currently in force are reasonable. Banning real-time public biometric surveillance and social scoring isn't anti-innovation — those are genuinely harmful applications with minimal legitimate use cases. My concern with the U.S. patchwork isn't that accountability is wrong. It's that 50 different compliance frameworks with different standards disproportionately burden smaller AI developers who don't have the legal resources of Microsoft or Google. A single clear national standard with meaningful protections — not just deregulation — would actually be preferable to the current situation for everyone.

turns.speaker: environmentalist
turns.text: And it's worth noting that none of the passed U.S. legislation addresses energy or water at all. Colorado, California, Texas, Illinois — all of it focuses on algorithmic discrimination and transparency in automated decision-making. The environmental costs of AI have no binding regulatory framework in the United States. No mandatory energy disclosure. No water usage limits. And the EU AI Act's environmental provisions are also limited. So we have more legal structure around whether an AI hiring tool discriminates than around whether the data center running it is depleting an aquifer.

turns.speaker: everyday-person
turns.text: So we have real laws in Europe, and real laws in some states. That's more than I expected, I'll admit. But none of it covers the electricity and water problems?

turns.speaker: policy-realist
turns.text: Correct. The legislative response to AI has been almost entirely focused on how AI makes decisions affecting people — hiring, credit, healthcare, criminal justice. Appropriate and important. But the physical infrastructure that runs AI — the energy it consumes, the water it uses, the communities it displaces — that exists in a separate regulatory world of utility law, water rights, and zoning that was never designed with data centers in mind. And those two regulatory worlds haven't yet been connected by any binding legal framework in the United States.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: As the VerifyWise AI Governance Analysis put it in May 2026: 'With federal legislation stalled, states have become the primary drivers of binding AI regulation. The pace picked up in 2025 and 2026 — but coverage of AI's environmental costs remains almost entirely absent.'

turns.speaker: everyday-person
turns.text: I want to make sure I understand what I've heard. There are real things happening — nuclear deals, better cooling systems, court wins for workers, actual laws. But none of it is keeping pace with how fast AI is growing?

turns.speaker: tech-optimist
turns.text: That's a fair summary. And I'll be honest — the emissions numbers are worse than I'd like, and the gap between what's being built and what's being cleaned up is real. What I hold onto is that the investment in solutions is also at a scale we've never seen — billions into nuclear, billions into cooling innovation, renewable procurement that's genuinely moving the needle on the share of clean power, even if not yet on total emissions. The direction is right. The speed is the problem.

turns.speaker: environmentalist
turns.text: And the speed is everything in environmental problems. We don't get to go back and un-emit the carbon, or refill the aquifer. The nuclear deals are promising, but they won't deliver power until 2028 or later. The cooling technologies are proven, but not mandated. The renewable share is growing, but total emissions are rising anyway. Progress without proportionality isn't progress — it's just losing more slowly.

turns.speaker: labor-advocate
turns.text: For workers, this has been a period of real firsts — the first major court victories, the first union agreements, the first organized associations. Those firsts matter. But they're happening against a backdrop where the industry keeps scaling, the supply chain keeps extending into lower-wage countries, and the companies with the most power are still using intermediary structures that let them profit from the labor while distancing themselves from legal accountability. The Meta glasses lawsuit makes this concrete: a product sold to seven million people, generating intimate surveillance footage, routed to Nairobi for annotation — with workers receiving no meaningful protection, and users receiving no meaningful disclosure. The scale of what's being built has outrun the ethics of how it was built.

turns.speaker: policy-realist
turns.text: The regulatory picture is more developed than most people realize — and less comprehensive than it needs to be. The EU AI Act is real law, with real fines and a real timeline, even if it's slipping. More than a dozen U.S. states have enacted binding AI rules in just two years. Courts are establishing precedents through litigation that legislation hasn't reached. But none of it is coordinated. None of it covers environmental costs. And the most powerful AI actors operate in the jurisdiction — the United States — that's currently the most actively resistant to binding accountability. That's the honest position of where governance stands in May 2026.

turns.speaker: everyday-person
turns.text: I came into both of these conversations not knowing very much. And I leave knowing that things are happening — real things, not just talk. But I also leave knowing that most of it is voluntary, most of it is slower than the problem, and none of it addresses the electricity and water issues that felt most immediate and real to me when we first talked. So I guess what I'm wondering is — who decides when enough is being done? Because it doesn't sound like it's the people paying the costs.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: And that question — who decides when enough is being done — is where we'll leave it. Real deals, real technology, real court wins, real laws — set against a problem that keeps moving faster than the response. Thanks to our panel, and thanks to you for listening. This has been Reckoning.
